topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The knock on the door made my heart hammer against my ribs. It was late, the kind of quiet hour where every creak of the old building seemed amplified. I peered through the peephole, and my breath hitched. A large, brown box sat by the mat, sitting where my neighbor, Mrs. Henderson, used to leave her recycling. It was clearly addressed to the previous occupant, a faded script on the side. My palms began to sweat, the moisture chilling my skin despite the stuffy apartment air. I backed away from the door, my gaze darting to the hallway, half-expecting someone to materialize from the shadows. A low, tight sound escaped my throat as I fumbled for the deadbolt, my fingers clumsy and cold against the metal.

[story 2]

When the delivery person handed over the parcel, the silence in the hallway felt heavy, suffocating. It was a plain cardboard box, sealed with heavy tape, and the address label was unmistakable—the former resident’s name was scrawled in hurried, unfamiliar handwriting. I took the package, the cardboard rough against my fingertips, and immediately felt a tremor run through my legs. Every instinct screamed at me to drop it and flee. I stood rooted to the spot, my eyes wide, scanning the darkened corridor. The shadows seemed to deepen, coalescing into shapes that weren't quite right. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my throat making the effort painful, and desperately wanted to be anywhere else, anywhere but here, with this unknown object in my hands.

[story 3]

I found the package tucked behind the communal mailboxes. It was heavier than it looked, dense with an oppressive stillness. My stomach clenched, a tight knot tightening with every shallow breath I took. I tried to rationalize it—a misplaced delivery, perhaps—but the way the box seemed to absorb the weak hallway light unsettled me profoundly. I edged toward the entrance, my steps slow and deliberate, each footfall sounding unnaturally loud on the linoleum floor. My gaze kept flicking back to the box, a compulsion pulling me forward, yet simultaneously anchoring me in place. I gripped the edges of my jacket, my knuckles white, waiting for some sound, some indication that this was not merely a misplaced item.
